narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA right around the typical 3.21 average and your upper-middle family income background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on students' academic and background details, estimated a 63% chance that you would fail it—high enough to lead to the tough decision of rejecting your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3, both at the lowest level of 1, far below the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5, which strongly pushed the model toward forecasting bar failure by signaling early and persistent academic challenges compared to most applicants. Your LSAT score of 30.5, notably lower than the typical 36.7, also worked against you, though less intensely than your deciles, as it pulled the prediction somewhat toward failure rather than success. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.2 nearly matched the dataset average, offering a modest counterbalance that nudged the odds slightly toward passing, much like it does for many students. Similarly, your upper-middle family income provided a small positive influence, aligning with profiles that tend to fare better, and your choice of full-time study—shared by over 90% of students—added a minor boost toward the passing side. Overall, while features like your GPA, income, and full-time status helped a bit, they couldn't overcome the heavy weight of your low Year 1 and Year 3 deciles and below-average LSAT, which stood out as bigger hurdles than for typical applicants who rank higher in school performance. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman leader in law—many overcome similar starts through targeted growth. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, seeking academic support to improve your standing in advanced coursework, and perhaps exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your unique background. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing, and reapply stronger next cycle. We believe in your future success.
